title: ASEAN’s critical minerals and industrial-upgrade dilemma
gist: ASEAN holds dominant critical-mineral reserves (Indonesia >60% of global nickel supply) and aims to move from raw exports to high-value downstream processing, but China's near-total grip on refining, intra-ASEAN competition and steep financing requirements may turn that mineral wealth into a new form of dependence rather than industrial transformation.

themes.theme: ASEAN's critical-mineral endowment and downstream ambition

insights.claim: Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply and the Philippines is the world's largest nickel-ore exporter with an estimated US$170 billion in nickel deposits, making Southeast Asia central to high-tech supply chains.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Concentration of nickel supply in two ASEAN states is a structural lever over EV battery and stainless-steel supply chains; watch Indonesia/Philippines policy for price signals.
insights.quote: Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply. The Philippines is the world's largest nickel-ore exporter and possesses an estimated US$170 billion in nickel deposits.
insights.locator: Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply

insights.claim: ASEAN is pivoting from low-value raw-mineral exports toward higher-value downstream refining, processing and manufacturing, targeting electric vehicles, lithium-ion batteries, solar photovoltaics and semiconductors as high-growth sectors.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Forced downstreaming (export bans, processing mandates) reshapes where battery and EV value capture occurs; favors firms building processing capacity inside ASEAN.
insights.quote: The objective is to transition from low-value raw-mineral exports toward higher-value downstream processing and manufacturing within the global critical-minerals industry, which generally has higher economic outcomes for producing countries.
insights.locator: Critical minerals also present ASEAN with an opportunity

insights.claim: Global demand for critical minerals is likely to rise further as countries diversify energy sources, AI spreads and rearmament accelerates, with copper needed for energy-transmission grids and nickel for battery storage.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Structural demand tailwind from energy transition, AI data centers and rearmament supports a long-run bull case for copper and nickel.
insights.locator: Critical minerals are key to energy diversification

themes.theme: China's refining chokehold as structural vulnerability

insights.claim: China accounts for more than 90% of global rare-earth-element refining capacity and 60-70% for lithium and cobalt, so ASEAN's downstream ambitions may deepen rather than reduce its dependence on China.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: China's midstream dominance is the binding constraint on any Western or ASEAN supply-chain diversification; refining capacity, not mining, is the chokepoint to track.
insights.quote: China accounts for more than 90% of global REEs-refining capacity, and 60–70% for lithium and cobalt.
insights.locator: Industrial upgrading and increasing geo-economic risks

insights.claim: Chinese firms control around 75% of Indonesia's nickel-refining capacity, including via major investments in industrial complexes, creating a structural vulnerability for Indonesia and ASEAN.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Chinese ownership of Indonesian nickel refining means Western 'friend-shoring' of nickel faces entrenched incumbency; a key risk for non-China battery supply chains.
insights.quote: In Indonesia, Chinese firms control around 75% of the country's nickel-refining capacity, including via major investments in industrial complexes.
insights.locator: At the same time, several key midstream processing capacities

insights.claim: High geographical concentration of nickel, cobalt and copper production in Indonesia and the Philippines creates choke points where a single country can disrupt global supply and exposes the bloc to market shocks.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Single-country concentration is itself a risk vector; supply disruption in Indonesia or the Philippines would cascade across the bloc and global markets.
insights.locator: Vulnerabilities in the supply chain create choke points

insights.claim: Developing new mines and processing facilities involves long lead times and substantial long-term capital, making it hard for ASEAN to respond quickly to demand surges or supply cuts.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Long lead times mean supply responses lag demand, supporting tight-market pricing for critical minerals over the medium term.
insights.quote: Building end-to-end supply chains requires substantial long-term capital investment.

insights.claim: In May 2026 the Asian Development Bank launched new financing tools to help Asia-Pacific economies reduce supply-chain concentration, and Japan expanded financing via the Asia Zero Emission Community and Partnership on Wide Energy and Resources Resilience, later joined by the World Bank in June 2026.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Multilateral and Japanese financing tools are the capital channels enabling de-risking from China; watch their scale as a gauge of diversification momentum.

themes.theme: US-led alliances versus China and intra-ASEAN fragmentation

insights.claim: Following the launch of Pax Silica in December 2025 and the US Critical Minerals Ministerial in February 2026, the US signed bilateral critical-mineral deals with Indonesia, Malaysia and Thailand, with Australia, India, Japan, Singapore and South Korea as core participants in American-led initiatives.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: An emerging US-led critical-minerals bloc (Pax Silica) is the counterweight to China; alignment of ASEAN states is the swing factor for non-China supply chains.
insights.quote: Combined with recent US bilateral critical-mineral deals with Indonesia, Malaysia and Thailand, ASEAN will likely see new economic opportunities

insights.claim: US-led supply-chain frameworks may intensify geo-economic fragmentation within ASEAN as some members align with US initiatives while others prioritize ties with China, challenging regional cohesion.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Bloc fragmentation undermines ASEAN collective bargaining; individual-state alignment, not ASEAN consensus, will drive mineral-supply outcomes.
insights.locator: On the other hand, these developments may also intensify

insights.claim: Whether ASEAN's mineral wealth becomes a foundation for industrial transformation or a new form of dependence will ultimately depend on who finances, processes and integrates the region into next-generation supply chains.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The decisive battleground is midstream financing and processing control, not raw reserves; that is where to watch China-versus-US competition play out.
insights.quote: Whether ASEAN's mineral wealth becomes a foundation for long-term industrial transformation or a new form of dependence may ultimately depend on who finances, processes and integrates the region into the next generation of strategic supply chains.
insights.locator: ASEAN's minerals industry amid global tensions
